algorithms.name: Memoization (Top-Down DP)
algorithms.analogy: Sticky notes on a whiteboard — solve a subproblem once, stick the answer on the board. Next time you need it, just read the note instead of recalculating.
algorithms.story: A student calculating Fibonacci(50) the naive way calls Fibonacci(49) and Fibonacci(48). Fibonacci(49) calls Fibonacci(48) again. The same subproblems are called billions of times. Memoization is a sticky note on every computed answer — Fibonacci(50) now takes 50 additions instead of 2^50.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Problem has overlapping subproblems (same sub-call made repeatedly)
• Problem has optimal substructure (optimal solution built from optimal sub-solutions)
• You can naturally write a recursive solution
• State space is clear and finite
• Classic problems: Fibonacci, Longest Common Subsequence, 0/1 Knapsack, Coin Change
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Write the naive recursive solution first
2. Add a memo HashMap (or array) as parameter or instance field
3. At the start of each recursive call, check if memo contains the answer → return it
4. Compute the result normally (base cases + recursive calls)
5. Store the result in memo before returning
6. Top-down: subproblems are solved lazily (only if needed)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Always start with the recursive solution — memoization is just adding a cache to it
• State identification is the hard part: what parameters uniquely define a subproblem?
• For 2D state: use int[][] or HashMap<String, Integer> — avoid string keys (slow) for large n
• If interviewer asks to optimize space: switch to bottom-up DP (tabulation)
• Overlap check: draw the recursion tree — if you see repeated subtrees, DP applies
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Game AI Move Evaluation","desc":"Chess/Go engines evaluate millions of board positions using memoization — the same board position reached via different move sequences is only evaluated once."},{"title":"Compiler Optimization","desc":"Compilers use DP to solve register allocation — determining which variables to keep in CPU registers to minimize memory accesses."},{"title":"Bioinformatics Sequence Alignment","desc":"BLAST (genomic sequence alignment) uses memoized LCS variants to align DNA/protein sequences against databases."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Returning memo[n] without checking if it's been set — use -1 as sentinel, not 0 (which may be a valid answer)","Mutable state in memo keys — if your state includes an array, use a canonical string representation or sort first","Stack overflow on deep recursion — for n > 10000, consider iterative bottom-up DP instead","Using global memo across test cases — always reset the memo between calls in competitive programming"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Tabulation (Bottom-Up)","desc":"Fill a DP table iteratively from base cases upward — eliminates recursion overhead and stack overflow risk"},{"name":"Space-Optimized DP","desc":"If each state only depends on the previous row/column, use a 1D array rolling window to reduce O(n²) space to O(n)"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: DP = recursion + cache. Nothing more. Write the recursive brute force first. Then ask: 'Do I call the same subproblem twice?' If yes, cache it. The cache IS the memoization. Identify the state (what parameters uniquely define a subproblem) and you've solved 80% of DP problems.
algorithms.patternSignal: minimum/maximum of something, number of ways, can you reach, longest subsequence, count distinct ways, optimal strategy, overlapping subproblems, fibonacci pattern, knapsack, coin change
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Climbing Stairs","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Fibonacci in disguise — dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Your first DP."},{"name":"Coin Change","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"dp[amount] = min coins. For each coin, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1)"},{"name":"Longest Increasing Subsequence","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. Try all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]"},{"name":"0/1 Knapsack","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"2D DP: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w"},{"name":"Edit Distance","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Classic 2D DP — if chars match, dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]; else min of 3 operations"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["fib(5)"] --> B["fib(4)"]
    A --> C["fib(3) ← CACHED after first call"]
    B --> D["fib(3) ← compute, cache"]
    B --> E["fib(2)"]
    D --> F["fib(2)"]
    D --> G["fib(1)=1"]
    E --> H["fib(1)=1"]
    E --> I["fib(0)=0"]
    C -.->|"memo hit!"| D

algorithms.name: Tabulation (Bottom-Up DP)
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(n) · reducible to O(1) for 1D problems
algorithms.analogy: Building a staircase from the ground up — start at step 0, compute each step using the ones you've already built.
algorithms.story: Instead of memoization's 'lazy' approach (only compute what you need), tabulation is proactive: fill in every cell of a table starting from the smallest subproblems. No recursion, no stack overflow, and often faster due to cache locality. It's the same math, just computed in a different order.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Same as memoization: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
• Prefer tabulation when n is large (avoids stack overflow)
• When space optimization is needed (rolling array trick)
• When you need to know all subproblem solutions, not just the final one
• Classic: 0/1 Knapsack, Longest Increasing Subsequence, Edit Distance
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Identify the state: dp[i] means 'answer for subproblem i'
2. Set base cases: dp[0], dp[1], etc.
3. Fill the table in order from smallest to largest subproblem
4. Each cell is computed using previously filled cells
5. Return dp[n] (or the required final state)
6. Optional: reduce space by keeping only the last 1-2 rows
algorithms.interviewTips:
• 0/1 Knapsack: iterate weights BACKWARDS (high to low) to avoid using the same item twice
• LIS in O(n log n): use patience sorting / binary search — important optimization to mention
• Edit distance is the backbone of spell checkers, git diff, and DNA alignment — know it cold
• Rolling array trick: if dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1], use two variables instead of an array
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Spell Checker","desc":"Edit distance measures how many single-character edits transform one word into another — autocorrect suggestions are ranked by edit distance."},{"title":"Stock Trading DP","desc":"LeetCode's 'Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock' series are tabulation problems — dp[i] = max profit up to day i."},{"title":"Text Diff (git diff)","desc":"git diff computes the Longest Common Subsequence between two file versions to show what changed."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Wrong iteration direction in Knapsack: going forward allows an item to be picked multiple times (that's unbounded knapsack)","Not initializing base cases correctly — dp[0] is often the most critical cell","Off-by-one in string DP: dp[i][j] usually refers to first i characters of string A and first j characters of string B (1-indexed strings)","Assuming O(n) DP is always possible — 2D DP (LCS, Edit Distance) is O(m*n), not reducible below O(min(m,n)) space"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Unbounded Knapsack","desc":"Items can be used multiple times — iterate weights FORWARD (low to high) instead of backward"},{"name":"LIS in O(n log n)","desc":"Use a patience sorting array + binary search — replace the naive O(n²) DP with binary search on a maintained tails array"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph LR
    A["dp[0] = base case"] --> B["dp[1] = f(dp[0])"]
    B --> C["dp[2] = f(dp[1], dp[0])"]
    C --> D["dp[3] = f(dp[2], dp[1])"]
    D --> E["..."] --> F["dp[n] = answer"]
    style A fill:#1a4a1a
    style F fill:#4a1a1a

algorithms.name: 0/1 Knapsack
algorithms.analogy: Packing a hiking bag with a weight limit — for each item, choose to pack it or leave it to maximize total value.
algorithms.story: A thief breaks into a museum. The bag can hold 10 kg. There are 5 items with different weights and values. He can't take fractions of items. This classic NP-hard problem (brute force 2^n) becomes O(n*W) with DP — because the choice for item i only depends on the remaining capacity after choosing item i-1.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Subset selection with a capacity constraint
• Yes/no decision for each item (can't split)
• Partition problem (can we split array into two equal-sum subsets?)
• Target sum, subset sum, combination sum IV
• If items can repeat → Unbounded Knapsack
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. dp[w] = maximum value achievable with capacity exactly w
2. Initialize dp[0..W] = 0
3. For each item i (weight w_i, value v_i):
4.   Iterate capacity w from W down to w_i:
5.     dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w - w_i] + v_i)
6.   Backward iteration ensures item i is used at most once
7. Answer: dp[W]
algorithms.interviewTips:
• The backward loop is the key insight — it prevents reusing the same item in one pass
• Subset Sum = Knapsack where value == weight and we want dp[target] != 0
• Partition Equal Subset Sum = Subset Sum with target = sum/2
• Coin Change uses FORWARD loop (unbounded) — each coin can be used unlimited times
• If W or target is huge (> 10^6), consider meet-in-the-middle instead of DP
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Resource Allocation","desc":"Cloud providers solve knapsack-like problems to pack virtual machines onto physical servers — maximize utilization without exceeding capacity."},{"title":"Financial Portfolio Optimization","desc":"Choose which assets to include in a portfolio given a risk budget — maximize expected return subject to total risk constraint."},{"title":"Cutting Stock Problem","desc":"Manufacturing: given steel rods of various lengths, cut them optimally to fill orders with minimum waste."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forward loop in 0/1 Knapsack — this turns it into unbounded knapsack (items reused)","Not handling the case where target is unreachable (dp[target] remains at initial sentinel)","Confusing 0/1 Knapsack with Fractional Knapsack — fractional is solved greedily, not with DP","Array index out of bounds: ensure w >= weights[i] before using dp[w - weights[i]]"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Unbounded Knapsack","desc":"Items can be reused — iterate weights FORWARD. Use cases: Coin Change, Rod Cutting"},{"name":"Bounded Knapsack","desc":"Each item has a count limit k — can be reduced to 0/1 by binary grouping of item counts"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["For each item i with weight w and value v"] --> B["For capacity c from W down to w"]
    B --> C{"dp[c-w] + v > dp[c]?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["dp[c] = dp[c-w] + v"]
    C -- No --> E["Keep dp[c]"]
    D --> F["Next capacity"]
    E --> F
    F --> B
    B -->|"All capacities done"| G["Next item"]
    G --> A
    A -->|"All items done"| H["Answer: dp[W]"]
